zh-hans.lifeSummary: Kotian Khan of the Kumans是Konchek Otrakovich Khan Of The Cumans的儿子，于1155年出生在俄罗斯Смоленская ОбластьСмоленский районСмоленск。他至少有2个儿子。他去世于1240年享年85岁。

nameMeanings.name: Khan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Arabic/Muslim Mohammad, Mohammed, Abdul, Muhammad, Mohamed, Bibi, Tariq, Ali, Asif, Khalid, Amir, Saeed.

Muslim (also common among African Americans): from khan, an English spelling of a status name based on Mongolian khaan ‘(military) ruler, sovereign’. This was originally a hereditary title among Tartar and Mongolian tribesmen (in particular Genghis Khan, 1162–1227), but is now very widely used throughout the Muslim world as a surname. In Iran and parts of the Indian subcontinent it is used as an honorific title after a person's name.

variants.name: Khanom
meanings: Muslim (Bangladesh and Iran): from Persian khānum ‘princess, noblewoman’. This is not actually a surname, but a female honorific, the feminine equivalent of Khan . Compare Khanam .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
Chinese:
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: the
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Vietnamese: unexplained.
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 鄭, possibly based on its Hokkien or Teochew pronunciation, see   Zheng   1.

variants.name: Zheng
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Jian, Yi, Bin, Hong, Hua, Li, Wei, Yong, Ting, Xin, Yan, Hui, Min, Hao, Shen, Da, Quan, Tian, Chong.
Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 鄭: from Zheng (鄭), the name of a state (located in part of present-day Shaanxi and Henan provinces) granted to You, the younger brother of King Xuan of Zhou (reigned 827–782  BC ). The state of Zheng was annexed by the state of Han (located in present-day Shaanxi and Henan provinces) during the Warring States period (475–221  BC ).

nameMeanings.name: Kotian
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Czech (mainly Kocián) and Slovak (also Kocián): variant of Kocan .
Americanized form of Slovenian Kocjan or its mostly Croatian variant Kocijan, and possibly also a shortened form of the Slovenian patronymics Kocjančič and Kocijančič, Croatian Kocijančić.
Americanized form of Hungarian Kóczián: patronymic from the personal name Kocz, a short form of Konstantin (see Constantine ).

variants.name: Kocan
meanings: Slovak (Kočan): from a pet form of archaic koča ‘cat, kitten’ (see Koca ).
Polish; Czech and Slovak (also Kocán): from a pet form of the personal name Chocimir (Polish), Chotimír (Czech and Slovak).
Turkish (Koçan) and Bosniak (Kočan): nickname from Turkish koçan, Bosnian kočan ‘stump or hard stalk of cabbage’, also ‘(bare) corncob’.

variants.name: Kocjan
meanings: Slovenian: from the personal name Kocjan, a vernacular form of Kancijan, Latin Cantianus, the name of a Christian martyr in S. Canzian d'Isonzo (Slovenian Škocjan) in Friuli, in northeastern Italy, under the Roman emperor Diocletian. See also Kocian 2.

en.lifeSummary: Kotian Khan of the Kumans was born in 1155, in Smolensk, Smolensky Rayon, Smolensk, Russia as the son of Konchek Otrakovich Khan Of The Cumans. He had at least 2 sons. He died in 1240, at the age of 85.

fr.lifeSummary: Kotian Khan of the Kumans est né en 1155, à Smolensk, Smolenskiy Rayon, Smolensk, Russie, fils de Konchek Otrakovich Khan Of The Cumans. Il a eu au moins 2 fils. Il est décédé en 1240, à l’âge de 85 ans.
